data.title: Hershey Trust rejected Mondelez offer as too low - Bloomberg
data.description:
Hershey Co.'s (NYSE:HSY) controlling shareholder, the Hershey Trust, rejected Mondelez's (MDLZ) preliminary offer as too low.

The philanthropic trust, which has ~80% of the voting power at the chocolate maker, rejected the Mondelez (MDLZ) offer, according to a Bloomberg report on Wednesday, which cited people familiar with the matter. CNBC's David Faber reported the news earlier.

Hershey shares fell 5% on Wednesday after Mondelez (MDLZ) announced a $9 billion share repurchased authorization and said that it's focused on bolt-on acquisitions.

Hershey (HSY) shares jumped 11% on Monday after Bloomberg reported that Mondelez (MDLZ) was evaluating a potential takeover of the chocolate maker. Mondelez made a preliminary approach to Hershey about a possible deal.

"Mondelez always and forever will want to buy Hershey, but the questions is when will the timing be right in terms of the Hershey Trust's willingness to enter sincere negotiations that conceivably would result in a deal," CNBC's David Faber said earlier Wednesday, citing a person involved in the matter.

Hershey rejected a takeover approach from Mondelez (MDLZ) in 2016 as the Hershey Trust was against a transaction.

data.title: S&P, Nasdaq snap two-day decline as latest inflation data cements Fed rate cut bets
data.description:
U.S. stocks were mostly higher on Wednesday, after an in-line consumer inflation report cemented expectations of another quarter-point rate cut by the Federal Reserve next week.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) and the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) halted a two-day slide each, with the former +0.67% at 6,075.37 points in morning trade and the latter +1.33% at 19,948.86 points. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) wavered, last -0.01% at 44,244.80 points.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was up 2 basis points to 4.24%, while the shorter-end 2-year yield (US2Y) was down nearly 1 basis point to 4.14%.

Earlier in the day, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics said the consumer price index (CPI) for November rose 0.3% M/M, its highest reading since April and matching the consensus estimate. On a Y/Y basis, headline CPI climbed 2.7%, also matching expectations. Meanwhile, core CPI remained steady for a fourth straight month at +0.3% M/M and came in-line. It increased 3.3% on a Y/Y basis, also in-line.

Ahead of the Fed's final monetary policy committee meeting of the year next week, the CPI data showed that there is still work to do for policymakers in terms of getting inflation back down.

"Today's inflation data probably do not represent a sea change in the outlook for the Federal Reserve. Over the course of 2024, inflation has continued to slow, albeit more gradually than many had hoped it would at the start of the year," Wells Fargo's Sarah House said.

"We believe the FOMC will continue to reduce the federal funds rate next year in an effort to move monetary policy to a less restrictive position. We look for 100 bps of rate cuts from the FOMC over the next 12 months, with 25 bps rate cuts at next week's FOMC meeting and the March, June and September meetings next year," House added.

The U.S. bull run had stalled over the past two days ahead of the November CPI data. Still, Wall Street is on track to end another year with hefty gains.

Wednesday's spotlight was also grabbed by a few key earnings reports. Macy's (M) slid -7.5% after the department store chain delivered a quarterly decline in comparable sales and cut its annual adjusted profit per share guidance.

Heavily shorted GameStop (GME) added +6.3%, after the video game retailer delivered a surprise quarterly profit.

Adobe (ADBE) stock will be in focus ahead of the creative software maker's results after the closing bell.

data.title: Lilly reports positive Phase 3 breast cancer data for imlunestrant
data.description:
Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) reported positive Phase 3 data for its drug candidate imlunestrant as both a combination and monotherapy in the treatment of advanced breast cancer.

The study called EMBER-3, showed imlunestrant significantly reduced the risk of progression or death by 38% compared to standard endocrine therapy in patients with ESR1 mutations. It also demonstrated that imlunestrant in combination with Lilly’s (LLY) Verzenio significantly reduced the risk of progression or death by 43%, compared to imlunestrant alone, in all patients, regardless of ESR1 mutation status, according to a statement.

Imlunestrant is being evaluated in patients with ER+/HER2- advanced breast cancer, whose disease progressed on a prior aromatase inhibitor, with or without a CDK4/6 inhibitor.

Overall survival results for the study were not mature at the time of analysis. The study will continue to assess overall survival as a secondary endpoint, Lilly said.

The data will be presented Wednesday at the 2024 San Antonio Breast Cancer Symposium and published simultaneously in the New England Journal of Medicine.

data.title: Crude inventory fell 1.4M barrels for week ended Dec 6 - EIA
data.description:
• Commercial crude stocks for the week ended December 6: 422.0M barrels.
  • Crude inventory change: -1.4M barrels vs. -5.1M barrels for the week ended November 29. Consensus estimate -1.000M.
  • Gasoline inventory change: 5.1M barrels vs. 2.4M barrels for the week ended November 29. Consensus estimate -0.200M.
  • Distillates inventory change: 3.2M barrels vs. 3.4M barrels for the week ended November 29. Consensus estimate 0.900M.
  • SPR: 0.7M barrels vs. 1.4M barrels for the week ended November 29.
  • Futures (CL1:COM) +1.17% to $69.39 a barrel.
  • ETFs: (USO), (UCO), (SCO), (BNO), (DBO), (USL).

data.title: Principal Financial, Baldwin Insurance lowered to Underweight at Wells Fargo
data.description:
Wells Fargo Securities downgraded Principal Financial Group (NASDAQ:PFG) to Underweight from Equal-Weight as analyst Elyse Greenspan sees pressures on organic growth in the company's Retirement and Income Solutions business, among other negative developments.

For next year, Greenspan expects poor organic growth in RIS, chiefly driven by withdrawals. "We expect the outflows to weigh on fee income, but natural fee compression to have a larger impact," she wrote in a note to clients.

Similarly, she projects Principal Global Investors, Principal's (NASDAQ:PFG) asset management unit, to log muted organic growth for 2025, owing to higher withdrawals in both equity and fixed income.

"We acknowledge that PFG has reiterated that it is prioritizing revenue and margins over net cash flows for certain business segments but continued net outflows would weigh on the stock, especially with the lack of clarity or guidance from mgmt on a potential recovery timeframe," the note said.

In a separate note, the analyst also cut The Baldwin Insurance Group (NASDAQ:BWIN) to Underweight on the expectation that the company will face EBITDA pressures in 2025, due to elevated reinsurance costs. Other negative catalysts include softer margin expansion vs. peers and slowing organic growth amid rate and exposure headwinds.

In Wednesday morning trading, (PFG) drifted down 1.2% and (NASDAQ:BWIN) retreated 1.3%.

Seeking Alpha's peer page compares numerous metrics of (PFG) and (BWIN).

data.title: Bipartisan group to push for break up of pharmacy-benefit managers - WSJ
data.description:
A bipartisan group of lawmakers is expected to introduce legislation to break up pharmacy-benefit managers.

Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D. Mass) and Josh Hawley (R, MO) will propose a bill that would force companies that own health insurers or pharmacy-benefits managers to sell off their pharmacy business, according to a WSJ report on Wednesday. A companion house bill was also expected to be introduced on Wednesday.

The three biggest PBMs include CVS Health's (NYSE:CVS) Caremark, Cigna's (NYSE:CI) Express Scripts and UnitedHealth's (NYSE:UNH) OptumRx. Shares of CVS fell 4% on Wednesday, while Cigna dropped almost 5% and UnitedHealth dropped 5.4%.

"PBMs have manipulated the market to enrich themselves -- hiking up drug costs, cheating employers, and driving small pharmacies out of business, " Warren said, according to the WSJ. "My new bipartisan bill will untangle these conflicts of interest by reining in these middlemen."

The bipartisan bills come after the Federal Trade Commission filed suit against Optum Rx, Caremark Express Scripts in late September, accusing the trio of inflating the prices of insulin drugs to boost profits.

The FTC in July released a report as part of an investigation into pharmacy benefit managers (PBMs), accusing them of profiting at the expense of patients and independent pharmacies, thus contributing to higher drug prices.

data.title: T. Rowe Price AUM rises 3.7% in November as markets gain
data.description:
T. Rowe Price (NASDAQ:TROW) preliminary assets under management increased to $1.67T at Nov. 30, 2024, from $1.61T at Oct. 31, with surging equity markets offsetting preliminary net outflows of $6.4B.

Equity AUM rose 5.1% to $859B, during a month in which the S&P 500 climbed 5.6%.

Fixed income, including money market, AUM edged up 0.5% to $187B.

Multi-asset AUM of $568B increased 3.1% M/M.

Year-to-date, T. Rowe Price's (NASDAQ:TROW) preliminary AUM rose 15%.

data.title: Microsoft AI chief hires former Google DeepMind staff for AI health unit: report
data.description:
Microsoft's (NASDAQ:MSFT) CEO of AI, Mustafa Suleyman, has hired multiple former employees of Google's (NASDAQ:GOOG)(NASDAQ:GOOGL) DeepMind to help run Microsoft's new consumer artificial intelligence health division, according to a report by Financial Times.

Suleyman, a British entrepreneur who co-founded DeepMind in 2010, was hired by Microsoft in March 2024 to run its AI unit. Suleyman is now building Microsoft's AI health division with former DeepMind staff, including Dominic King, former head of DeepMind's health unit, and Christopher Kelly, a former clinical researcher at DeepMind, as well as two others, according to the report.

Health care has blossomed as a key driver for the AI revolution. It is not only being used in research and development, but also on the consumer side, managing health data and running hospital and clinical administrative tasks.

A survey released earlier this month by Deloitte found 62% of consumers are willing to use chatbots for personal medical advice and 55% for mental health issues.

data.title: Albertson's terminates merger, sues Kroger for breach of agreement - update
data.description:
Updated 10:05ET with a response from Kroger on Albertson's lawsuit.

After a federal judge ruled in favor of the Federal Trade Commission and agreed to grant a preliminary injunction to block the proposed $25B merger between Albertson’s (NYSE:ACI) and Kroger (NYSE:KR), Albertson’s (ACI) has officially terminated its efforts to merge with the grocery chain and has sued Kroger (KR) for breach of the merger agreement.

Additionally, Albertson’s (ACI) says it is now entitled to the $600M termination fee.

“Given the recent federal and state court decisions to block our proposed merger with Kroger, we have made the difficult decision to terminate the merger agreement. We are deeply disappointed in the courts’ decisions,” Albertson’s CEO Vivek Sankaran said early Wednesday.

Albertson’s (ACI) is pointing the finger at Kroger (KR) for its failure to offer an adequate divestiture package and repeatedly ignoring regulators’ concerns about creating an anti-competitive environment. The lawsuit, filed in the Delaware Court of Chancery, seeks damages of billions of dollars to “make Albertson’s shareholders whole.”

“Rather than fulfill its contractual obligations to ensure that the merger succeeded, Kroger acted in its own financial self-interest, repeatedly providing insufficient divestiture proposals that ignored regulators’ concerns,” Albertson’s general counsel Tom Moriarty said in a statement, confirming that the basis of the lawsuit is supported by rulings from the District Court of Oregon and King County Superior Court in Washington.

Kroger (KR) swiftly responded to Albertson's (ACI) lawsuit, insisting the claims are "baseless and without merit," and the company worked on the merger "with integrity and commitment."

"Albertson's is seeking to deflect responsibility following Kroger's written notification of Albertson's multiple breaches of the agreement," Kroger (KR) said in a statement cited by Bloomberg, adding that the company is not entitled to a breakup fee.

The effort to merge the two grocery chains has been plagued by regulatory resistance from the very beginning. The FTC stepped in to block the merger in February, arguing that a combination of the two would be anti-competitive and lead to higher prices for consumers. Eight states and Washington, D.C. joined in the fight to block the deal, prompting Kroger (KR) to make concessions to satisfy regulators, including the sale of 600 of its stores to C&S Wholesalers. However, in her decision, Oregon judge Adrienne Nelson said the divestiture was not sufficient in scale to adequately compete with the merged firm.

Lastly, while the merger would have resulted in a $5.2B windfall for Cerberus Capital Management, the firm said it does not intend to sell its stake in the company and remains confident in its strength as a standalone company.

data.title: Lilly to sell Zepbound through telehealth platform Ro
data.description:
Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) plans to streamline patient access to its weight-loss drug Zepbound by offering single-dose vials through telehealth platform Ro.

The vials will be made available through an integration with Lilly’s (LLY) LillyDirect self-pay pharmacy channel. The drugs will be prescribed by a Ro-affiliated healthcare provider, according to a Ro statement.

News of the Ro partnership appeared to weigh on Hims & Hers Health (HIMS), with shares sliding 5% in morning trading. Hims (HIMS) has been offering a compounded version of Zepbound, also known as tirzepatide, though its telehealth website.

Shares of Lilly (LLY) were down 1% Wednesday morning. The company also reported data for its breast cancer drug candidate imlunestrant.
